TextENGNew: ...Grandfather's papers were defiled.
changeReason: JPN 穢された means 'defiled/desecrated,' not 'trampled'; new translation corrects the verb to convey sacrilege.

TextENGNew: We have evidence as clear as the very pathogen causing Hinamizawa Syndrome—so why can they not accept its biological behavior?
changeReason: Old says 'life cycle' but JPN 生態 means 'biological behavior/ecology'; new translation uses the correct scientific concept.

TextENGNew: ...Because they've never heard of such a thing?
Because they cannot imagine it?
Because something so absurd could not possibly be real...?
Don't tell me that...
I could not care less about the limits of those mediocrities' comprehension.
Why won't anyone believe the truth of Grandfather's papers, which the Irie Institute has been substantiating to this very day?
And why do people insist on believing that human beings are somehow special?
Humans are simply one species among many animals.
While numerous parasites that manipulate their hosts have been documented, how can anyone declare with certainty that no such parasite targets humans?
...No, wait—that's not the issue.
That's not the issue at all.
Because until now, everyone had believed Grandfather's papers...
Wasn't it precisely because they were impressed by his work that the various ministries offered their cooperation?
changeReason: Old specifies 'Ministry of Health and Defense Agency' while JPN 各省庁 means 'various ministries' generally. Also old says 'lent me their assistance' (first person) while JPN says cooperation was offered to 'him' (third person). Minor specificity issue.

TextENGNew: ...Yet they turned on me overnight, as if flipping a switch.
In other words, all of their support had been thanks to Uncle Koizumi's patronage.
...So does that mean nobody ever seriously read Grandfather's papers?
Did they merely pretend to value his work to curry favor with Uncle Koizumi?
Then what has everything I've built up until today amounted to...?
I felt as though I had accomplished something—but what have I truly achieved?
...I no longer understand anything, and all I can do is drown in sorrow.
The more I think, the less I understand the meaning of my own life.
Takano... no... Miyo Takano was supposed to live her life being useful to Grandfather, wasn't she...?
changeReason: New translation preserves the self-correction 'Takano...no...Miyo Takano' from JPN, reflecting the narratively important distinction between married and birth name.

TextENGNew: ...If so, then I have accomplished nothing.
To count three alongside Grandfather, and then presume to count four on my own—what insufferable arrogance...
changeReason: Old 'crazy thing to say' vs new 'insufferable arrogance'; JPN おこがましい means presumptuous/arrogant, a self-critical tone the old translation missed.

TextENGNew: If I leave even the smallest space for thought, self-criticism and doubt about my very existence will flood in...
Each time I felt the cracks in my heart beginning to open, I poured in more alcohol to fill them, if only slightly.
changeReason: Old changes tense from past habitual ('each time I felt cracks open') to future intention ('I'm just going to'). Also loses 'only slightly' nuance.

TextENGNew: ...Is it truly so absurd to anticipate the existence of something that can influence human thought and behavior?
Admittedly, Hinamizawa Syndrome itself does not directly give rise to ideology.
At most, it can drive patients to delirium.
Socialism and capitalism do not spring from it.
The era in which Grandfather lived was one where the world could not be discussed without reference to ideology.
Since his papers were born of that era, some exaggeration or bias might be conceded—but even so, that is no reason to deny their prescience.
...It should not be dismissed with a single scoff of 'that's ridiculous.'
And the most critical finding of all—the one they ignored—is the fact that Rika Furude, the queen carrier, holds the fate of the entire village in her hands.
From the very beginning of his Hinamizawa Syndrome research, Grandfather foresaw the existence of the queen carrier, as well as the importance and danger she posed.
But the hypothesis that the death of the queen carrier would trigger a catastrophic collapse of the infected colony is a Pandora's box that cannot be proven.
It can only be verified after Rika Furude's death—and by then, it would be far too late.

...However, the objective fact that Rika Furude is a special existence known as the queen carrier is beyond dispute,@k and numerous experiments have demonstrated that she exerts some unique influence upon the general infected population.
Her death could truly serve as the trigger for the collapse of the entire village.
...Of that, there can be no mistake...!!
Because Grandfather foresaw it—he wrote it!
To call that a fantasy out of a comic book... that is simply too cruel...!
To label the various preparations we made to avert that worst-case scenario—her protection and contingency measures—as a sham project tunneling funds... that is just too...
What I am doing is carrying on the work Grandfather intended to do.
Therefore, to insult my research is to insult Grandfather himself.
In his papers, Grandfather stated that the very first priority when researching the syndrome should be the protection of the queen carrier.
That should be an indisputable fact, the most essential directive—yet it was the thing they mocked the most...!!
changeReason: Old omits the important first clause establishing what was mocked as an indisputable, essential truth. Captures 'they ridiculed it most' but loses crucial context.

TextENGNew: The more I recall, the more those humiliating words come flooding back.
...I can almost still hear their voices, and I cannot resist the urge to cover both ears...
...The board members surely think I've lost my mind.
I had intended to petition for the continuation of the Irie Institute... but it backfired completely.
Now the board must consider Hinamizawa Syndrome even more of a joke, and they will push harder than ever to terminate the research.
If that happens, I will be reduced to private research once again.
In other words, the same position Grandfather was in.
...There are severe limits to what personal passion alone can accomplish in research.
...No, it's doubtful I would even be allowed to return to private research.
When the Irie Institute is disbanded, all personnel will be bound to lifelong secrecy regarding every operation they were involved in.
I signed a consent form to that effect.
...This is not a mere verbal agreement. If I were to break the terms, a security unit like the Mountain Dogs would eliminate me to maintain the secrecy.
changeReason: Old softens 抹殺してでも秘匿する (eliminate to maintain secrecy) to 'come after me to ensure confidentiality' — the consequence is euphemized but the direction (punishment for breaking promise) is preserved.

TextENGNew: ...Even setting that aside.
The Irie Institute plans to eradicate Hinamizawa Syndrome within the next few years and bury all traces of it, including the research findings.
This is not research and treatment.
changeReason: Old 'isn't the normal process of research' vs new 'This is not research and treatment at all'; JPN flatly denies it qualifies as research.

TextENGNew: ...It is nothing more than a cover-up—pretending none of it ever existed.
...I had been determined to inherit and complete Grandfather's research... but in reality, the opposite is happening.
...I am actually helping to erase Grandfather's cherished life's work from this world.
A treatment has already been developed.
Irie is despicable—he shows no enthusiasm for any other aspect of the research, yet pours passion into the treatment that rivals even my own.
changeReason: Old omits 卑劣にも (despicably) and presents Irie's selective passion neutrally — different emotional register that loses Takano's contempt, but the factual observation is preserved.

TextENGNew: ...And, loath as I am to admit it, his track record and talent are more than sufficient.
...He will undoubtedly draft a plan to eradicate Hinamizawa Syndrome within three years, exactly as the new board demands.
...And the board will certainly allocate ample funding—solely for the purpose of burying the syndrome.
And Irie will gladly bury even the fact that Hinamizawa Syndrome ever existed.
changeReason: Old truncates to just 'Irie will do that happily' while JPN specifies he will 'gladly bury even the fact that Hinamizawa Syndrome ever existed.' Plot-critical detail omitted.

TextENGNew: ...Just as he so readily destroyed all the H170 test reagents and research materials on the board's orders...!
All I can do is watch from the sidelines as Irie and the others eradicate Hinamizawa Syndrome, passively hindering them in the same way they hindered me.
And I will continue writing a paper that can never be published, one that will inevitably be ordered destroyed when everything is over...
changeReason: New adds that the paper 'can never be published' (JPN 永遠に発表できず), a key detail missing from old translation.

TextENGNew: ...Should I clutch that paper to my chest and throw myself off a cliff, so that I might at least present it to Grandfather...?
changeReason: Old vaguely says 'kill myself with thoughts of it' while JPN specifies 'clutch that paper to my chest and throw myself off a cliff.' The specific method is more dramatic but the suicidal ideation is conveyed either way.

TextENGNew: ...Grandfather would not be pleased even if I did.
...On the contrary, he would be appalled that I threw away the research he spent half his life nurturing.
...He would never welcome me into his embrace. Of that I am certain...
I wanted to present Hinamizawa Syndrome to the world.
I wanted the world to acknowledge it as a monumental achievement.
...I wanted to etch Grandfather's name into history... and elevate him to godhood.
Grandfather waited for that day, believing without wavering... and even after entering eternal slumber, he must still be yearning for it...
...And I... ruined it all...
I intended to make Grandfather a god.
I intended to become a god myself.
...If we did, we would be together forever, and I would never have to be alone again.
If I became a god, God could no longer test me.
No misfortune would come. No sudden accident would strike.
Grandfather would never leave me, nor would Uncle Koizumi.
...I would never have to go back to being alone...
...I am alone.
I have no allies.
I was merely dancing in a frenzy on a stage where no one was watching.
When I looked up, there was no applause—only ridicule.
...No, it's doubtful whether anyone was even in the audience to begin with...
My parents died, my grandfather died, and then Uncle Koizumi died...
Three times, God has toyed with me by rolling the die.
Each time, I thought I had overcome the trial through my own strength—but I was wrong.
...Each time, I had merely been protected by the next guardian to come along.
And now, there is no guardian left to protect me...
Where has it gone—that fierce spirit I had when I first came to Hinamizawa, ready to challenge God and knock Him from His throne...?
changeReason: Old is a declarative statement; new is a rhetorical question matching JPN どこへ行ってしまったのか = 'where has it gone.' Different sentence mood.

TextENGNew: Now, far from challenging God... I am nothing but a loser being toyed with...
Everything suddenly ceases to matter.
...If I allowed myself to care, the sadness would swallow me whole. So I keep soaking my heart in alcohol to make it all stop mattering...
A sleek black car pulled up abruptly beside me.
...The sight of it reminded me instantly of the car that had come to capture me when I escaped the orphanage, and my intoxication vanished in a heartbeat.
Emerging from the front and passenger seats were men in black suits and sunglasses, looking thoroughly suspicious.
...My first thought was immediate.
...They must be agents sent by one of the powers behind my clients.
changeReason: Old 'sent by one of my clients' vs new 'agents of the powers behind my clients'; JPN クライアントの黒幕たち specifies 'masterminds behind the clients.'

TextENGNew: I am involved in a research project so classified that its very existence must be erased,@k I am its key figure, and I am mentally unstable. Obviously they would not leave me at large.
changeReason: Old fabricates 'development of a biological weapon' while JPN describes 'a research project so classified that its very existence must be erased.' Specific fabrication.

TextENGNew: ...They must intend to abduct me, encase me in concrete, and erase my existence...
...Or so I thought.
I fully expected them to pin my arms behind my back and shove me roughly into the car.
...Having braced myself for that, I was somewhat taken aback when they bowed to me with utmost courtesy.
``You are the Vice Director of the Irie Institute, Major Miyo Takano, correct?``
``...Normally, yes. ...Right now, I'm just a drunken loser.``
``There is someone who would like to meet with you. Would you be willing to accompany us?``
``...Even if I refuse, you'll force me to come along anyway, won't you?``
I'll be killed regardless... and even if I lived, I could never fulfill Grandfather's wish...
I said it half in self-mockery.
changeReason: Old 'Cynical thoughts ran through my head' (thinking) vs new 'I said it half in self-mockery' (speaking); JPN 自嘲気味にそう言った = 'said it self-deprecatingly.'

TextENGNew: Then, the person seated in the back spoke.
``We won't force you. ...But I believe we can be of assistance to you, Major.``
She appeared to be close to my age.
changeReason: Swapped content: JPN says 'she appeared to be close to my age' but old says 'I've never met this young woman before' (next entry's content).

TextENGNew: But I had never met this young woman before.
changeReason: Swapped content: JPN says 'I had never met this young woman before' but old says 'She must be around my age' (previous entry's content).

TextENGNew: Since she knows about the Irie Institute, she must be connected to my clients in some way.
...Surely she is too young to be a client herself.
...Most likely she is an emissary sent by a client who prefers not to show their face.
...Even if I live, I cannot make Grandfather a god.
I cannot become a god myself either.
Then who cares where or how a loser like me is disposed of?
I steeled myself and climbed into the back seat they offered.
...Once I was settled, the luxury car glided into motion with characteristic smoothness.
For a time, silence filled the car.
...They were the ones who extended the invitation.
I had nothing to say on my end.
``...It's been a while, though you likely don't remember me. We crossed paths at Koizumi-sensei's funeral.``
``...Oh, we did? I'm sorry, I don't recall.``
The funeral had been enormously well-attended.
I cannot remember a single face.
...But what does it mean that she opens with that?
...Does it mean she belongs to the Koizumi faction...?

``Koizumi-sensei served for many years as one of the opinion leaders guiding Japan's postwar reconstruction. It would be difficult to discuss the peace and prosperity of modern Japan without acknowledging his contributions.``
``When we were rebuilding from the scorched fields after the war, our nation's pioneers were united as one. Regrettably, that unity no longer holds.
...As the era of peace lengthened and a generation with no memory of those scorched fields began entering the corridors of power, those who understood our founding ideals steadily dwindled.
Koizumi-sensei was the last elder among those noble comrades, the central pillar who was to guide our nation into the twenty-first century.``
``...After his death, the Koizumi faction fell swiftly. Other factions rushed in to fill the void, and things have become rather... chaotic, haven't they?``
I had heard about this from Jirou-san, so I was already aware.
And it is no different from the feudal era.
When a lord or great general dies, a succession crisis inevitably follows, plunging the realm into turmoil.
...Our country has not changed in hundreds of years.
``Exactly. ...Unfortunately, while peaceful Japan has been building a comfortable society, it has also caused us to forget the noble ideals that guided us at the outset.

At present, Tokyo is consumed by ceaseless infighting among factions vying for the seat and privileges left behind by Koizumi-sensei. If Koizumi-sensei and the other fallen architects of our reconstruction could see this, they would surely weep.``
``...What does any of this have to do with me?``
``Are you not its greatest victim? I believe you've had more than enough experience with winds that shift without warning...?``
Indeed, the research was launched under Koizumi-sensei's patronage.
...And the hostility from the anti-Koizumi factions has been bitterly fierce.
...To them, perhaps every project Koizumi-sensei left behind is simply an eyesore, regardless of its substance.
...Which is to say... they did not actually reject the content of the research itself...?
changeReason: Old says 'they rejected the research itself' (affirming rejection) while JPN says 'they did not actually reject the content of the research itself' (denying rejection). Opposite conclusions.

TextENGNew: ...Come to think of it, something similar happened to Grandfather.
When Koizumi-sensei brought authorities from various fields to evaluate his work.
...They reversed their prior assessments and savaged Grandfather without hesitation.
changeReason: Old 'talked down to' vs new 'reversed their prior assessments'; JPN 手の平を返した means they reversed/backtracked on previous praise, not merely condescended.

TextENGNew: ...Koizumi-sensei later told me that behind-the-scenes pressure from the shadowy powers of that era was responsible...
``It is no exaggeration to say that the Hinamizawa Syndrome research is the crown jewel of the Alphabet Project in terms of budget, infrastructure, and significance.
As such, your research was made into a scapegoat by those who seek to monopolize the Project's interests.``
``...A scapegoat...``
``In other words, the Hinamizawa Syndrome research itself was not what they rejected.
What matters to them is not the project's content, but which faction controls it. That is what I wanted to convey to you today, Takano-san.``
``...In the end, I am just a sacrificial lamb put on display... It doesn't matter what I research. The only thing they care about is that this was a Koizumi faction project...``
``Precisely. Factions are the wind itself. Sometimes it blows in your favor; sometimes against you. Unfortunately, once the wind shifts, turning it back is no simple matter.``
``You're saying it won't be easy to reverse my clients' hostility...?``
``Correct. The Alphabet Project has been completely taken over. Every director has been replaced, and they make decisions solely to enrich themselves.
...Your impassioned appeal never stood a chance—they had no intention of listening from the start.``
...I dug my nails into my knees, remembering that day of humiliation...
``...Let me change the subject for a moment. What is the true purpose of your research, Takano-san?``
``Huh? ...Well, intellectual curiosity as a researcher, I suppose...``
``Wasn't it the promise you made with the late Dr. Takano—the man you revere as your grandfather...?``
...I was startled.
Koizumi-sensei had cautioned me that revealing this could prove disadvantageous, and I had never mentioned it to any of my clients.
...How did she come to know?
If they know that, then they may know far more about us than I had imagined.
``Where did you learn that?``
``From Koizumi-sensei himself.``
``What?!``
``We are not your enemies. Please, be at ease. We came here because we truly wish to help you.``
I don't know who she truly is, but there can be no doubt she came seeking something from me.
...What exactly does she intend to negotiate?
Knowing even the relationship between Grandfather and me, I could not afford to let my guard down.
``However, for us to help you, we need you to be completely honest with us. In other words, we need you to lay your cards on the table.``
``...I don't understand what you mean.``
``What is your true purpose in researching Hinamizawa Syndrome?``
...To have Grandfather's research recognized as a monumental achievement... and to elevate him to godhood.
``Isn't it to force the world to acknowledge the late Dr. Takano's research—and to exact revenge upon those who trampled and scorned the papers your grandfather poured his heart into writing?``
I could barely keep the shock from showing on my face.
How much does this woman know about me?
...No—who on earth is she?
How can she read my every thought as though it were written in plain sight?
But there is no way I can admit it aloud.
...One must maintain appearances...
Yet this woman with her elegant smile—wearing the expression of an angel—laughed with the whisper of a devil, outmaneuvering me by several measures.
There is a difference between a smile and a smirk.
A smile conveys goodwill... but a smirk means the exact opposite.
This woman has read to the very bottom of my heart, and now she is measuring how far I am willing to be honest.
Whether I speak the truth is no longer the question.
...She is only testing whether I can demonstrate enough trust to confide it.
``If I am completely mistaken, please forgive me. We'll drive you to whichever station you prefer. The nearest would be Gogura Station, perhaps?``
``But. ...If what I have said comes even close to how you truly feel... then I believe we can become partners who help one another. What do you think? Miyo Takano-san?``
I cannot form words in reply, but this silence already carries the same meaning as an answer.
...Because I want to hear what she has to offer.
Until today, I had obscured my purpose behind the abstract phrase 'making Grandfather a god.'
But... she has just illuminated my true objective for me.
My purpose was... revenge for Grandfather's bitter defeat.
I want them to scramble over each other to read the papers they once scorned and trampled.
I want them to believe every last word Grandfather left behind—to worship it.
That is what my true purpose has always been...
And this woman spoke aloud what I had only ever murmured within my own heart, then asked me if she was correct.
...Is this woman an angel... or a devil?
...If I am human, then is she something beyond...?
``Your misfortune was failing to understand your true objective in concrete terms. That is why, no matter how long you continued your research by inertia, you could never reach it.
You set an abstract goal—'uncovering the mysteries of Hinamizawa Syndrome'—one that could never be achieved. And so you were left questioning the meaning of your own life.
...Isn't that so? Hee hee. Yes, that's the wrong goal. Your real objective—no, your dream—is far less abstract than that.
Can you picture it? Imagine this scene: the true apex of power in this nation, reading your grandfather's papers, astonished by their contents, believing every word without a shred of doubt.
...They reverently handle those papers—the ones with footprints on the back—and tremble before the prescience of his research. ...Well? Can you see it...?``
...That was something I had never imagined before.
...And yet, it may have been my dream all along...
``Your grandfather coined the term 'Hinamizawa Syndrome,' yes? ...That name will resound in every corner of this nation, and the words he created will be etched into eternity.``
``Etched into eternity... for all time...``

``Yes. It would mean your grandfather—his achievement—would be elevated to an eternal existence. ...Hee hee hee. Isn't that the dream to which you've devoted your life as Miyo Takano...? Hee hee hee...``
Grandfather's achievement, etched into eternity... enduring forever.
Enduring.
...Forever...
``And yet!! The swine who cannot comprehend that achievement are trying to sacrifice your dream on the altar of their petty factional squabbles and power grabs, trampling it without mercy!
...Just as they trampled your grandfather's papers... beneath their boots. ...See? Hee hee hee...``

``You are seething with frustration. The materials you poured your heart into are the same as the papers your grandfather devoted his soul to writing. You cannot forgive those who laughed at them and ground them into the dirt.

You cannot forgive the swine who mocked your grandfather's life's work. Am I right? ...Of course I am. But you, in your kindness, simply didn't know the words to condemn them. You never arrived at that thought on your own.``
An emotion I cannot name surges within me... I can barely breathe.
Wariness and relief, trust and suspicion, all swirl together.
...My heart has begun pounding without my noticing, and my fingertips tremble restlessly.
Why... do I feel relief?
Because she has given voice to the dream I could never articulate myself, even at this age.
And because she has given voice to my directionless fury.
Why... do I feel doubt?
Because she has revealed what I have never confided to anyone,@k no—what I have never even confided to myself.
``Hey... Miyo-san? If what I've said is not merely 'not wrong,' but... perfectly, exactly right... then I believe I can help you realize both your dream and your revenge.``
``Hee hee, is something the matter? Are you feeling unwell? If so, I shouldn't impose any further. We'll take you to the nearest station.``
A tiny voice spilled from my lips, pathetically.
...It was like a child caught in a prank, unable to hold back the confession any longer.
``...Did you say something? Hee hee.``
...She must have heard me. Yet the woman smiled, coaxing me to say it once more.
``...That's not it... I'm not... unwell...``
``Hee hee hee. I'm glad. Shall we continue, then?``
``...If that truly is my dream... then what can you possibly do?``
``I will assist you with both of your acts of revenge.
The first is revenge on behalf of your grandfather. We will compel those who mocked his papers to take them reverently into their hands once more—those very papers they trampled underfoot.
The second is revenge against the board of directors—those who, motivated by nothing more than factional rivalry, crushed the research that embodies the bond between you and your grandfather.
Revenge against the swine who stomped on your work before your very eyes, and sought to stomp on your life as well.``
``...Have them take Grandfather's papers into their hands once more... and exact revenge on the board that defiled them...``
``To be equally frank: we are helping you because our interests align.
We cannot stand by while the project Koizumi-sensei established for Japan's future is devoured by parasites after his death.
If we had more power, we would never have allowed such people to interfere. But the winds have shifted, and the Project can no longer embody Koizumi-sensei's ideals.
It has degenerated into a sty for swine to fatten themselves on. Allowing such a project to continue would not be what Koizumi-sensei, who gave the Alphabet Project its very name, would wish.
The Project should have been dissolved the moment it departed from his ideals.``
``...So you intend to turn the sacrificial lamb of the factional wars into a wolf?``
``If you would prefer to retire with your grandfather's paper hidden in your heart, never to be published, we will not force you.``
...Anything but that.
I myself may fall to ruin—that I can accept.
But at the very least... Grandfather...
At least Grandfather's papers...
``Don't you want to make the paper that represents you and your grandfather's combined lifework into something eternal...? Hee hee—after all, isn't that your dearest wish...?``
``...Yes... That is... my reason for living...``
``We will help you seize the chance to make your true dream a reality.``
...On the night I accepted that I was life's loser, abandoned by all, drowning in tears and alcohol,@k I received the aid of a being I could not tell was angel or devil...
Was it heaven's mercy, or the devil's whisper...?
I had fought the new board of directors for the continuation of the research.
But... that had been entirely beside the point.
...It had nothing to do with my true purpose.
My purpose is not to continue researching Hinamizawa Syndrome.
...It is to force the world to acknowledge the research Grandfather conducted.
...Somewhere along the way, the means had become the end, leading me astray...
My life's purpose was not to carry on Grandfather's research.
...It was to avenge his bitter defeat—to take revenge.
The woman is saying it.
She is promising to make Grandfather's research eternal.
And she is even promising an opportunity for revenge against those who laughed at it.
When Uncle Koizumi died, I believed God had once again toyed with me by rolling His die.
But... my unyielding will summoned a power that transcends the roll of any die.
...Perhaps this encounter was inevitable.
...Curse you, God—testing me with Your die yet again...
I will overcome Uncle Koizumi's death.
And I will surely, surely make Grandfather an eternal being, and take revenge upon the God who dared to test me...
Without fail...!
I will have my revenge.
